title: The Backwards Blossom Machine
synopsis: When Thistle's invention to make cherry blossoms bloom faster works in reverse, he and his friend Dewdrop find themselves bouncing between absurd alternate realities where everything in the orchard goes hilariously wrong. A first-person tale told through lists and mishaps in 1920s rural America.

chapters.title: My Perfectly Imperfect Invention
chapters.content:
The contraption exploded at exactly three-fifteen on a Tuesday, which I definitely didn't expect but probably should have.

I suppose I ought to explain why I built the thing in the first place, though my reasons seemed much more sensible before everything went sideways. My name is Thistle, and I live in the Pemberton Cherry Orchard in the spring of 1924, when humans still believed fairies were just stories their grandmothers told.

The orchard stretches for acres in every direction, row upon row of cherry trees that burst into pink blooms each April. The petals drift down like sweet-scented snow, carpeting the grass between the rows. Old Mr. Pemberton tends to his trees with the devotion of someone who talks to plants and expects them to listen back. The air always smells of honey and bark and that green smell of growing things.

My List of Why I Built the Backwards Blossom Machine (which wasn't backwards when I started, obviously):
1. The cherry blossoms only last two weeks
2. Dewdrop gets sad when they fall
3. I thought I was clever with springs and gears
4. Mrs. Pemberton mentioned the blossoms were late this year
5. I definitely didn't read the instructions on the Temporal Acceleration Coil properly

Dewdrop is my best friend, a rose petal fairy who lives three trees over. She has translucent pink wings and wears a dress made from actual rose petals that somehow never wilt. She's also much better at reading instruction manuals than I am, which I found out too late.

The machine itself was a masterpiece of fairy engineering, if I do say so myself. I'd built it inside a hollow at the base of the oldest cherry tree, using clockwork mechanisms I'd borrowed (without permission) from Mr. Pemberton's grandfather clock, springs from mousetraps, and a peculiar glowing crystal I'd found buried near the roots.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk sits surrounded by clockwork pieces, springs, and gears inside a cozy tree hollow. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle working on his invention inside the warm, lamp-lit tree hollow

chapters.title: Dewdrop's List of Concerns (Which I Ignored)
chapters.content:
"Are you absolutely certain this won't turn the cherry blossoms into tiny elephants?" Dewdrop asked, hovering near my left ear as I made final adjustments to the machine.

I definitely should have paid more attention to her question, but I was busy being proud of my engineering skills. The Backwards Blossom Machine hummed with an almost musical quality, its gears turning in perfect synchronization. The crystal at its heart pulsed with soft blue light.

Dewdrop's List of Things That Could Go Wrong (which she presented to me on a leaf):
1. Temporal displacement
2. Reality fractures
3. Accidental elephant transformation
4. The machine working backwards
5. Cherry trees growing upside down
6. Mr. Pemberton discovering we exist
7. Everything

"Your list is unnecessarily pessimistic," I told her, though I must admit item number four made me pause. "Besides, what's the worst that could happen?"

This is what humans call 'famous last words,' though I didn't know that at the time.

The morning air was crisp with the promise of spring, dewdrops clinging to every blade of grass. The cherry trees stood in neat rows, their branches heavy with tight buds that would soon burst into the pink clouds I was determined to hurry along. Mr. Pemberton was in his farmhouse, probably eating breakfast and completely unaware that two fairies were about to accidentally tear holes in the fabric of reality.

"At least let me double-check the crystal calibration," Dewdrop suggested, but I was already reaching for the activation switch.

"Trust me," I said, which turned out to be remarkably poor advice.

The switch clicked with a satisfying snap, and for exactly two seconds, everything worked perfectly. The machine hummed, the crystal glowed brighter, and I felt tremendously pleased with myself.

Then the humming became a whistling, the whistling became a shriek, and the world turned inside out.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk reaches for a switch while a rose petal fairy hovers nearby looking worried. The contraption glows ominously in the cozy tree hollow. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle about to activate his machine while Dewdrop watches with concern

chapters.title: The First Reality (Where Everything Grew Backwards)
chapters.content:
When my eyes stopped spinning in circles, I found myself staring at the most peculiar sight: cherry blossoms growing INTO the trees instead of OUT of them.

The petals were flowing upward like pink rivers, streaming from the grass toward the branches in graceful arcs. Each blossom folded itself neatly back into a tight bud, and the buds shrank until they disappeared entirely. It was quite beautiful, actually, if you enjoyed watching time run in reverse.

"Thistle," Dewdrop said in the tone she uses when I've done something spectacularly wrong, "what exactly did you do?"

"I think," I said, watching a particularly enthusiastic cluster of petals dive-bomb a tree branch, "the machine worked backwards."

My List of Things That Were Now Backwards:
1. Cherry blossoms (obviously)
2. The morning dew (rolling uphill)
3. A confused robin (flying tail-first)
4. Mr. Pemberton (walking backward to his mailbox)
5. Gravity (working sideways)
6. My confidence in my engineering abilities

The orchard had become a wonderland of reversed physics. Dewdrop and I watched in fascination as Mr. Pemberton backed his way to the mailbox, reached inside, and somehow managed to un-read his mail before walking backward to his house. A family of rabbits hopped in reverse across the grass, their cottontails leading the way.

"This is actually quite entertaining," I admitted, dodging a particularly aggressive cherry blossom that seemed determined to plant itself.

"It's also completely wrong," Dewdrop pointed out. "What happens when the seasons start running backwards? What if winter comes in the middle of spring?"

Before I could answer, the machine gave another tremendous shriek, reality folded like a paper airplane, and we tumbled into somewhere else entirely.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk and a rose petal fairy watch in amazement as pink cherry blossoms flow upward from the ground to the trees like reverse waterfalls. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Cherry blossoms flowing backwards while Thistle and Dewdrop observe the impossible sight

chapters.title: The Second Reality (Where Size Made No Sense)
chapters.content:
The second reality was immediately problematic because I was now the size of a small elephant, while Dewdrop had shrunk to the size of a pinhead.

"CAN YOU HEAR ME?" I called down to where I assumed she was, somewhere near my enormous left foot.

"Stop shouting!" came a voice so tiny I had to strain my now-gigantic ears to hear it. "You're giving me a headache!"

The cherry trees in this reality were behaving more sensibly, blooming at their proper size, but everything else had gone completely mad with proportions. A ladybug the size of a dinner plate landed on a flower petal the size of a dinner table. Earthworms thick as garden hoses poked their heads up from holes big enough to fall into.

My List of Size-Related Problems:
1. I couldn't fit in our tree hollow
2. Dewdrop was too small to operate the machine
3. A normal-sized ant was now terrifying
4. My voice was apparently loud enough to wake the dead
5. Walking anywhere might squish something important

Mr. Pemberton emerged from his house, took one look at a fairy the size of a small elephant standing in his orchard, and promptly fainted. I felt terrible about this, even though he'd probably convince himself it was a dream.

"How are we going to fix this?" I called to Dewdrop, trying to whisper but still sounding like distant thunder.

"The machine!" her tiny voice piped back. "But I can't reach the controls!"

I attempted to operate the delicate mechanisms with fingers now the size of bananas. This went about as well as you might expect. I managed to knock over two trees, accidentally step on the machine (which fortunately was sturdier than it looked), and somehow get my giant hand stuck in a bird's nest.

The machine, clearly fed up with my incompetence, decided to activate itself. Reality hiccupped, and we tumbled onward.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk, now giant-sized, carefully tries to operate tiny clockwork while a pinhead-sized rose petal fairy shouts instructions from below. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Giant Thistle delicately trying to work his tiny machine while minuscule Dewdrop directs him

chapters.title: The Third Reality (Where Everything Was Upside Down)
chapters.content:
In the third reality, I discovered that flying while everything is upside down is significantly more complicated than you might think.

We were back to our normal sizes, which was a relief, but now the entire orchard was hanging from the sky like a chandelier. The cherry trees dangled their roots in the air while their blossoms pressed against what I supposed was now the ceiling. Dewdrop and I found ourselves standing on clouds, looking down at the inverted world below.

"I'm getting dizzy," Dewdrop announced, and I had to admit the upside-down cherry blossoms were making my head spin too.

Below us (or above us?), Mr. Pemberton was going about his morning routine as if hanging upside down from the sky was perfectly normal. He walked along the undersides of clouds, tended his inverted trees, and somehow managed to drink coffee that flowed upward into his cup.

My List of Upside-Down Complications:
1. Which way was actually up?
2. How was Mr. Pemberton not falling into the sky?
3. Were we standing on the clouds or hanging from them?
4. Why did the coffee flow up but we didn't fall down?
5. Was I thinking about this too hard?

The machine, now dangling from a cloud like a strange fruit, continued its relentless humming. I was beginning to suspect it was enjoying these reality-hopping adventures far more than I was.

"Maybe," Dewdrop suggested as we watched a confused butterfly try to figure out which way was up, "we should have read those instruction manuals more carefully."

"Hindsight," I replied, reaching for the machine's controls while hanging upside down from a cumulus cloud, "is remarkably unhelpful right now."

The activation crystal pulsed once, twice, and reality spun like a top, depositing us somewhere entirely new and equally impossible.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk and a rose petal fairy stand on fluffy white clouds, looking down at an upside-down cherry orchard hanging from the sky. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle and Dewdrop standing on clouds above an inverted orchard

chapters.title: The Fourth Reality (Where Time Moved Sideways)
chapters.content:
The fourth reality was by far the most confusing, because time was moving sideways instead of forward, which meant everything was happening at once while simultaneously never happening at all.

I watched myself build the machine while also watching myself fix it while also watching myself break it, all occurring in the same moment but spread out like a fan across space. Dewdrop existed as seven different versions of herself, each one representing a different moment, having a heated discussion about what we should do next.

"This is giving me seven different headaches," all the Dewdrops said in unison.

"At least you're having the same headache seven times," I replied to my past, present, and future selves. "I'm experiencing every mistake I've ever made simultaneously."

My List of Sideways Time Problems:
1. I kept having conversations with myself
2. The cherry blossoms were blooming, falling, and growing back all at once
3. Mr. Pemberton was both awake and asleep
4. Everything that had happened was still happening
5. My brain felt like it was melting sideways

The orchard stretched in directions that didn't have names, with cherry trees that existed in every season simultaneously. Spring blossoms fell as autumn leaves while winter snow covered summer fruit. Mr. Pemberton walked through his orchard in all four seasons at once, somehow managing to be both young and old, both surprised and unsurprised by the chaos surrounding him.

"The machine!" Future Dewdrop called to Past Me. "You need to adjust the temporal flux!"

"What's temporal flux?" Present Me asked Past Dewdrop.

"Something you should have learned before building the machine," all seven Dewdrops replied with identical exasperation.

I reached for the machine with hands from three different time periods and somehow managed to flip a switch that existed yesterday, today, and tomorrow. Reality snapped back like a rubber band, and we fell into the next impossible place.

illustrations.description: Multiple versions of a tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk exist in the same space, along with seven versions of a rose petal fairy, all overlapping like transparent photographs. Cherry trees show all four seasons simultaneously. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Multiple overlapping versions of Thistle and Dewdrop existing in sideways time

chapters.title: The Fifth Reality (Where Everything Was Made of Jelly)
chapters.content:
The fifth reality was undoubtedly the wobbliest, as everything in the entire orchard had been transformed into various flavors of jelly.

The cherry trees were now cherry jelly, naturally, which made a certain logical sense until you realized they were also transparent and jiggly. Walking across the grass—now lime jelly—was like trying to navigate a dance floor made of gelatin. Every step sent ripples through the ground, which sent ripples through the jelly trees, which sent their jelly blossoms bouncing like tiny trampolines.

Dewdrop, who had somehow become rose petal jelly while maintaining her ability to fly, was having far too much fun bouncing off the jelly branches. "This is actually quite pleasant!" she called, doing a loop-de-loop through a strawberry jelly cloud.

My List of Jelly-Related Observations:
1. Everything tasted much better than expected
2. Walking was now essentially swimming
3. The machine had become clockwork jelly (still functional)
4. Mr. Pemberton was now coffee-flavored and seemed quite content about it
5. I was afraid to find out what I tasted like

Mr. Pemberton, now a pleasant coffee-jelly color, continued his morning routine with remarkable adaptability. He bounced rather than walked to his mailbox, which had become orange marmalade, and somehow managed to read letters that were now made of translucent raspberry jelly.

"How is he so calm about all this?" I wondered aloud, accidentally taking a bite out of a fence post (vanilla, surprisingly good).

"Maybe," Dewdrop suggested while doing backflips through a grape jelly puddle, "people are more adaptable than we think."

The machine, now a fascinating combination of mechanical precision and wobble, began its familiar humming. Even as jelly, it was determined to continue our interdimensional tour. I reached for the controls with fingers that squished rather than gripped, and we bounced our way into the next reality.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk bounces across a landscape entirely made of colorful, translucent jelly, including jelly trees with jelly cherry blossoms. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle navigating a whimsical jelly orchard where everything bounces and wobbles

chapters.title: The Sixth Reality (Where Everyone Was Extremely Polite)
chapters.content:
The sixth reality appeared completely normal at first glance, until everyone started apologizing for absolutely everything.

"I'm terribly sorry for existing," a cherry blossom said as it fell near my feet. "I do hope I haven't inconvenienced you by blooming."

"Not at all!" I replied automatically. "Please, don't worry about it!"

"You're too kind," the blossom replied. "I'll try to decompose as quietly as possible."

Dewdrop landed on a branch, which immediately began apologizing for not being more comfortable. "I'm so sorry I'm not more cushioned," the branch said. "Perhaps you'd prefer a different branch? I quite understand if I'm not suitable."

"You're lovely," Dewdrop assured it. "I'm sorry for being such a burden."

"Oh no, you're no burden at all! I'm sorry for suggesting you were!"

My List of Things That Apologized to Me:
1. The grass (for being too tall)
2. The wind (for ruffling my cap)
3. A confused bee (for buzzing too loudly)
4. My own shadow (for following me around)
5. The sunshine (for being too bright)
6. The machine (for malfunctioning)

Even Mr. Pemberton had been affected by the politeness epidemic. He spent his morning apologizing to his coffee cup for drinking its contents, saying sorry to the ground for walking on it, and asking each cherry tree's permission before pruning it.

"Pardon me," he said to a tree, "but would you mind terribly if I trimmed this branch? I'm so sorry to impose."

"Not at all!" the tree replied cheerfully. "I'm sorry the branch isn't more conveniently located for trimming!"

"This is exhausting," I whispered to Dewdrop.

"I'm sorry you find it exhausting," she replied. "I'm sorry for making you whisper."

"I'm sorry for complaining!"

"I'm sorry for making you feel the need to apologize!"

The machine, apparently sorry for all the trouble it had caused, began humming what sounded distinctly like an apologetic tune. I reached for the controls, saying sorry to each button before pressing it.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk listens as cherry blossoms with tiny faces apologize while falling. Everything in the scene appears to have polite, bashful expressions. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle surrounded by apologetic cherry blossoms with small worried faces

chapters.title: The Seventh Reality (Where Everything Rhymed)
chapters.content:
The seventh reality was quite a feat, where every single word had to meet another word that sounded just the same, which made conversation quite a game.

"This is really rather hard," I said to Dewdrop in the yard. "Every word must find its mate, or conversation has to wait."

"I quite agree, it's strange indeed," Dewdrop replied with verbal speed. "But look around at all the trees, their blossoms dance in rhyming breeze."

She was right, of course. The cherry blossoms fell in patterns that matched the rhythm of our speech. When we spoke in couplets, they danced in pairs. When we attempted more complex rhyme schemes, the petals swirled in corresponding patterns across the orchard floor.

My List of Rhyming Complications (which was quite hard to compose):
1. Thinking took much longer now, when every thought must rhyme somehow
2. Simple words became a quest, to find the rhymes that worked the best
3. Mr. Pemberton looked confused, his normal speech could not be used
4. The machine hummed songs all day, in verse and chorus it would play
5. My brain was getting rather sore, from rhyming words forevermore

Mr. Pemberton stood among his trees, looking rather ill at ease. He opened up his mouth to speak, but only rhyming words could sneak past his lips, which made him frown and pace in circles all around.

"Good morning, trees so fair and bright," he finally said with some delight. "Your blossoms pink are quite a sight, they fill my heart with pure light."

The trees rustled back their reply: "Good morning to you, farmer guy! We're happy 'neath the morning sky, our blossoms dance for you and I!"

"I think I'm getting quite a knack for speaking in this rhyming track," I told Dewdrop with a grin. "But still I'd like to back to where our words don't have to match with care, and speech flows free as morning air."

The machine agreed with humming song, it felt this rhyming had gone on quite long enough for anyone, and so it sang until we spun into another world once more, through reality's revolving door.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk speaks with animated gestures while cherry blossoms swirl in rhythmic patterns around him and a rose petal fairy. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle and Dewdrop having a rhyming conversation as cherry blossoms dance in poetic patterns

chapters.title: The Eighth Reality (Where Colors Had Gotten Mixed Up)
chapters.content:
The eighth reality was a painter's nightmare, where every color had decided to be somewhere it definitely shouldn't be.

The cherry blossoms were bright blue, the sky was a cheerful pink, and the grass had turned a vivid shade of purple. Dewdrop's normally pink wings were now a brilliant orange, while my mossy-green skin had become an alarming shade of yellow. Even my chestnut husk cap was now bright red.

"I look like a traffic light," I complained, examining my yellow hands and red cap.

"At least you're not orange," Dewdrop replied, fluttering her pumpkin-colored wings. "I feel like a sunset that's gotten lost."

My List of Incorrectly Colored Things:
1. Blue cherry blossoms (quite pretty, actually)
2. Pink sky (made everything look like cotton candy)
3. Purple grass (hurt my eyes to look at)
4. Orange Dewdrop (she was handling it well)
5. Yellow me (deeply unsettling)
6. Green clouds (confusing to navigate under)
7. Red tree bark (made the orchard look like it was blushing)

Mr. Pemberton, who was now bright green from head to toe, seemed remarkably unperturbed by the color chaos. He went about his morning routine, watering his trees with purple water from a blue watering can, occasionally pausing to admire the blue blossoms against the pink sky.

"Perhaps," he said to himself in a voice that was somehow also colored (a pleasant shade of lavender), "this is what the world looks like to butterflies."

A butterfly landed on his green nose—a butterfly that was, naturally, the wrong color entirely. It was bright red with silver spots, and seemed just as confused about the color situation as the rest of us.

"Do you think," Dewdrop asked, her orange wings catching the light from the pink sky, "that this is actually an improvement?"

I considered this, looking around at the orchard that now resembled a box of crayons that had melted together. "It's certainly more interesting," I admitted. "Though I do miss being green."

The machine, now a fascinating combination of colors that didn't have names, hummed its familiar tune and prepared to whisk us away once more.

illustrations.description: A yellow-skinned thorn fairy with pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a bright red cap made from a chestnut husk stands next to an orange-winged rose petal fairy in an orchard where blue cherry blossoms bloom against a pink sky above purple grass. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle and Dewdrop in their wrong colors surrounded by a completely color-swapped orchard

chapters.title: Finding Our Way Home (With a Little Help From Lists)
chapters.content:
The ninth reality was blessedly, wonderfully, completely normal, except for one small detail: the machine was finally working exactly as I had originally intended.

The cherry blossoms were blooming faster than ever, bursting from their buds in great clouds of pink and white. New blossoms appeared as quickly as the old ones fell, creating a constant shower of petals that never ended. The orchard hummed with the sound of accelerated growth, and the air was thick with the sweetness of eternal spring.

"It's working!" I shouted to Dewdrop, who was back to her proper pink color and looking tremendously relieved.

"Yes," she agreed, "but is it working too well?"

She had a point. The cherry trees were now blooming so enthusiastically that Mr. Pemberton was knee-deep in petals, and more were falling every second. The poor man stood in his orchard, watching his trees produce a year's worth of blossoms in a single morning, with an expression of complete bewilderment.

My Final List (The Most Important One):
1. Sometimes the best invention is knowing when to stop
2. Dewdrop's warnings are always worth listening to
3. Instruction manuals exist for good reasons
4. Not all problems need to be solved with machines
5. The cherry blossoms were beautiful just as they were
6. Friendship is more valuable than any invention
7. Home is wherever your best friend is, in any reality

I looked at the machine, humming proudly as it created more cherry blossoms than any orchard could possibly need, and made a decision that surprised even me.

"Dewdrop," I said, "I think it's time to turn it off."

"Really?" she asked, hovering near my ear. "But it's finally working the way you wanted."

"Actually," I realized, watching Mr. Pemberton try to wade through the sea of petals, "I think I wanted the wrong thing. The blossoms were perfect the way they were. Two weeks of beauty is enough—it's what makes them special."

I reached for the machine's controls one final time, but instead of adjusting the settings or trying to fix anything, I simply turned it off.

The humming stopped. The accelerated blooming ceased. The orchard settled into its natural rhythm, with just the right number of cherry blossoms drifting down in the morning breeze.

We were home.

illustrations.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk reaches toward his contraption's off switch while a rose petal fairy watches. They're surrounded by the perfect amount of gently falling cherry blossoms in golden morning light. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
illustrations.alt: Thistle turning off his machine while normal cherry blossoms fall peacefully around him and Dewdrop

coverPrompt.description: A tiny thorn fairy with mossy-green skin, pointed ears, wings like dried leaves, and a cap made from a chestnut husk stands next to a bizarre contraption made of twigs and flower petals. Pink cherry blossoms swirl in impossible spirals around them. Art style: indoor cozy scenes, pillow forts, sleepovers, hot chocolate, warm amber lighting, comfortable clothes, quiet moments, intimate friendship, blanket nests, fairy lights.
coverPrompt.alt: Thistle the thorn fairy with his backwards blossom machine surrounded by swirling cherry petals

questions.question: What was Thistle's original goal with his invention?
choices: To make the cherry blossoms last longer
To make the cherry blossoms bloom faster
To change the color of the blossoms
To make the trees grow taller
questions.explanation: Thistle built the machine to make the cherry blossoms bloom faster because they only lasted two weeks and he wanted to help them along.

questions.question: What did Dewdrop warn Thistle about in her list of concerns?
questions.explanation: Item #4 on Dewdrop's list specifically warned that the machine might work backwards, which is exactly what happened.

questions.question: In which reality did time move sideways?
questions.explanation: In the fourth reality, time moved sideways, causing multiple versions of Thistle and Dewdrop to exist simultaneously.

questions.question: What happened to everything in the fifth reality?
questions.explanation: In the fifth reality, everything in the orchard was transformed into various flavors of jelly, including the cherry trees and even Mr. Pemberton.

questions.question: How did Thistle ultimately solve the problem with his machine?
choices: He asked Dewdrop to fix it
questions.explanation: Thistle realized the cherry blossoms were perfect as they were and simply turned the machine off, accepting that two weeks of beauty was enough.

questions.question: What color were the cherry blossoms in the reality where colors were mixed up?
questions.explanation: In the eighth reality where colors were mixed up, the cherry blossoms were bright blue instead of their normal pink color.

questions.question: What important lesson did Thistle learn about friendship?
choices: Friends should always agree with each other
Dewdrop's warnings are worth listening to
questions.explanation: Thistle learned that Dewdrop's concerns and warnings were always worth listening to, and he should have paid attention to them from the start.

questions.question: What did Thistle realize about the cherry blossoms at the end?
choices: They were perfect as they were
They should be a different color
questions.explanation: Thistle realized that the cherry blossoms were perfect just as they naturally were, and that two weeks of beauty was what made them special.
